name: Khawla bint Hakim and Uthman ibn Madhun
title: Khawla bint Hakim and Uthman ibn Madhun (RA): The Righteous Couple
meta_description: The story of Khawla bint Hakim and Uthman ibn Madhun (RA), one of the first couples to accept Islam. A man the world could not touch, and a woman who joined the Prophet's household together.
keywords: Khawla bint Hakim, Uthman ibn Madhun, early Muslims, first companions, matchmaker of the Prophet, al-Baqi, sahaba stories
lede: They were not from the great tribes of Makkah. They held no banner that frightened anyone. Yet when the count is made of the first to believe, both of their names sit near the very top of the list.

This is the story of Khawla bint Hakim and Uthman ibn Madhun (may Allah be pleased with them): a husband who would not let the world touch him, and a wife who quietly shaped the household of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ.

paras: Some scholars place Uthman ibn Madhun and Khawla bint Hakim as the fourteenth and fifteenth people to accept Islam. This is the earliest of days, when believers could still be counted on a few hands.

They came from the smaller clans of Quraysh, not the powerful houses. Being of Quraysh gave them a measure of cover, but not the heavy protection the larger tribes could throw around their own. To believe, for them, was to step into the open with little to shield them.

When Uthman accepted Islam, his household followed. His brothers Abdullah and Qudamah believed, and his sister Zaynab too. Zaynab was the wife of Umar ibn al-Khattab, which made Uthman the maternal uncle of Abdullah ibn Umar and Hafsa (may Allah be pleased with them all). One narration says a whole group of them entered Islam in a single hour, then went home and brought their families in after them. For years afterward, that small circle stayed bound to one another.

sections.h2: The man who would not lose his mind
paras: Almost everything in the days of ignorance ran on wine. People drank at their festivals, at their gatherings, even in their worship and their funerals. To refuse it entirely was nearly unheard of, even among the nobles. Aisha (may Allah be pleased with her) would later say that the people of Makkah were so attached to it that, had it been forbidden outright at the start, they could not have borne it.

Uthman was one of the very few who simply would not touch it. When they asked him why, his answer was not about religion, for no command had yet come. It was about dignity. Why, he said, would I drink something that would take away my mind, and let those beneath me in character laugh at me while I make a fool of myself?

It is a small line, but it tells you who he was before a single verse reached him. He guarded his mind and his manners when nothing obliged him to, on the strength of his own sense of what a person should be. He was honest in trade, gentle in dealing, and loved even by people who would never share his faith.

When the persecution grew, the Prophet ﷺ chose Uthman to lead the first migration to Abyssinia, the small band of believers who left their homes to find safety in a Christian land. They came back when a rumour reached them that Makkah had accepted Islam. It had not.

Uthman was loved even by people who hated the message. Al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah, a man the Quran names for his hostility to the Prophet ﷺ, treated Uthman like a nephew and placed him under his protection. No one in Makkah would dare touch him.

But Uthman watched the poor and the weak among the believers being tortured, while he walked safe. It troubled him. He went to al-Walid and asked him to lift his protection. I want to feel what they feel, he said. I want to be with those who are being persecuted.

Soon after, in a gathering near the Kaaba, a famous poet recited that every blessing must one day fade. Uthman corrected him: not Paradise, that blessing never fades. A man struck him hard in the eye and injured it. Al-Walid offered his protection back. Uthman refused. The protection of Allah, he said, is better than yours. His only regret was that the other eye had not been struck too, for the sake of Allah.

sections.h2: The two greatest matches she ever made
paras: Khawla accepted Islam the moment Uthman came home with it. She grew close to Khadijah (may Allah be pleased with her) and to the Prophet ﷺ, and became a trusted presence in their home. She was known for her intelligence and her knowledge of medicine, and above all as a matchmaker: the woman who arranged marriages in Makkah, before Islam and after it. People trusted her with this delicate work because they trusted her judgement and her heart.

When Khadijah passed away, the house of the Prophet ﷺ fell into grief. Khawla kept quietly bringing food and checking in, and in time she was the one who gently told him it was time to marry again. He was hesitant, still bound to Khadijah's memory. She suggested two people. One was Sawda bint Zamaa (may Allah be pleased with her), a widow who would bring warmth and light back into a grieving home. The other was Aisha (may Allah be pleased with her), whom she described as the daughter of the dearest person in the world to him, Abu Bakr.

The Prophet ﷺ did not seize the moment for himself. Though he could have married whomever he wished, he answered with humility: mention me to their families, and let us see. Khawla took the task all the way through, speaking to Sawda and her father, then to the parents of Aisha, arranging both matches with patient care.

Think of what flowed from that. Every hadith we carry from our mother Aisha, every word she preserved of the Prophet ﷺ, traces back in part to a woman who simply brought two people together. It may be that Allah writes a share of all that good for Khawla.

sections.h2: The verse that filled his heart
paras: By now the couple had made three migrations for the sake of Allah: twice to Abyssinia, and finally to Madinah. In Madinah, Uthman's devotion deepened until he wanted to leave the world behind entirely: to fast every day, pray every night, and turn away from married life altogether. The Prophet ﷺ came to know of it when Khawla visited Aisha in worn old clothes, looking neglected, and explained that her husband no longer had any need of this world.

The Prophet ﷺ called Uthman in and asked, do you not have in me a good example? I fast and I break my fast, I pray and I sleep, and I keep the company of my family. Uthman answered that he was not turning from the Prophet's way, and returned to a balanced life. Soon Khawla came to Aisha again, dressed well and at ease, and said she was now treated as a woman should be.

One day Uthman was sitting with the Prophet ﷺ when he saw him raise his eyes to the sky, then look right and left, as if listening to something unseen. The Prophet ﷺ, who was always fully present with whoever sat before him, had turned to something else entirely. Jibril had come, he told Uthman, and revealed a verse. It was the verse recited from every pulpit on Friday since:

ayah.text: God commands justice, doing good, and generosity towards relatives and He forbids what is shameful, blameworthy, and oppressive. He teaches you, so that you may take heed.

sections.h2: The dunya could not touch him
paras: Uthman said that in that moment faith overwhelmed his heart, and he loved the Prophet ﷺ with a love he had not known before. This was the man who had already given up his protection, who had wished both his eyes struck for Allah, who had migrated three times. And still his certainty grew. Nearness to Allah, his life shows, has no ceiling; the heart that keeps being nourished keeps drawing closer.

He did not live long in Madinah. He was the first of those who had migrated from Makkah to die there, and the first of them to be buried in al-Baqi. The Prophet ﷺ was at his bedside as his soul left him, and when the tears came he leaned down and kissed Uthman on the forehead. From this the scholars take the gentle permissibility of kissing a loved one after death. He placed a stone to mark the grave, called it the grave of my brother, and said he would bury beside him those of his own family who died. So Uthman lies today among the family of the Prophet ﷺ in al-Baqi.

Over him the Prophet ﷺ said words that have never been forgotten: may Allah have mercy on you, Uthman; the dunya took nothing from you, and you took nothing from it. The world, the scholars explain, can take a person's character and faith from them by degrees. It never took anything from Uthman. Khawla wept for him in verse, glad tidings to a loved one now living in the pleasure of his Lord. Later a woman dreamt of a river flowing toward Uthman, and the Prophet ﷺ said: those are his good deeds.

sections.h2: A believing woman, named by Allah
paras: After Uthman died, Khawla was left alone in Madinah. She offered herself in marriage to the Prophet ﷺ. He did not marry her, as he did not marry several women who came to him, yet the way the Quran refers to such a believing woman who offers herself was understood to point to Khawla.

Scholars drew a quiet honour from this: Allah described her as a believing woman, praising her faith. She lived on as a narrator of hadith and a teacher of the next generation. Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz (may Allah have mercy on him) learned from her and called her al-Imra'a al-Salihah, the righteous woman.

From her we still carry the words of refuge taught by the Prophet ﷺ: I seek refuge in the perfect words of Allah from the evil of what He has created, said when you stop at a place, so that nothing will harm you until you leave it.

lessons_intro: Two people, one home, and two very different ways of carrying the same faith. Both are worth keeping.

lessons.bold: Guard yourself before you are told to.
lessons.text: Uthman kept his mind and his dignity intact when nobody required it of him. Good character is built in the small refusals long before the rules arrive.

lessons.bold: Do not hide behind your safety.
lessons.text: He gave up his protection because he could not bear to be comfortable while others suffered. Real solidarity costs the one who offers it.
Khawla brought two people together and never sought the credit. Some of the most lasting good we do is the kind no one applauds.
The dunya took nothing from Uthman, and he took nothing from it. A heart can stay clean if it never lets the world rearrange it.

faq.q: Who were Khawla bint Hakim and Uthman ibn Madhun?
faq.a: A married couple from Makkah and among the earliest people to accept Islam, some say the fourteenth and fifteenth. Uthman was a close and beloved companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, and Khawla was a trusted friend of his household.

faq.q: Why is Uthman ibn Madhun remembered?
faq.a: He refused alcohol before Islam, gave up his tribal protection to stand with the persecuted, and was the first of the Makkan migrants to die and be buried in Madinah. The Prophet ﷺ kissed his forehead and said the world took nothing from him.

faq.q: What was Khawla bint Hakim known for?
faq.a: She was known for her intelligence and her knowledge of medicine, and above all as a matchmaker. She arranged the marriages of the Prophet ﷺ to Sawda and to Aisha after the death of Khadijah, and later became a narrator of hadith and a teacher.

faq.q: Is this the same Khawla as al-Mujadila?
faq.a: No. Khawla bint Hakim, the wife of Uthman ibn Madhun, is often confused with al-Mujadila, the woman who pleaded with the Prophet ﷺ about her husband. They are two different companions.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Most of us will never be tested in front of a crowd. We are tested in quieter ways: whether we will stay comfortable while someone near us struggles, whether we can do good without needing it seen, whether the world is slowly rearranging our hearts without our noticing.

Uthman shows us a heart the world could not touch. Khawla shows us a kindness that asked for nothing and outlived her by centuries. Carry one of them into today: refuse one small thing that would dull you, or do one good thing no one will ever know about. That is their example, brought home.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Khawla bint Hakim and Uthman ibn Madhun (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). The Quran translation is from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Quran 16:90). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
They were not from the great tribes.

They held no banner that frightened anyone.

Yet both of their names sit near the top of the list of the first to believe.

Almost everything in those days ran on wine.

Uthman ibn Madhun simply would not touch it.

"Why would I drink something that takes away my mind, and let those beneath me laugh at me?"

A powerful man placed Uthman under his protection. No one would dare touch him.

Then he watched the weak being tortured while he walked safe.

So he gave the protection back.

He was struck hard in the eye for correcting a poet.

"The protection of Allah is better than yours," he said.

His only regret: that the other eye had not been struck too.

His wife Khawla was the matchmaker of Makkah.

When Khadijah passed and the house fell into grief, it was Khawla who arranged the Prophet's marriages to **Sawda** and **Aisha**.

Every hadith we carry from our mother Aisha traces back, in part, to a woman who simply brought two people together.

Quiet good can outlast you.

Uthman was the first of the Makkan migrants to die in Madinah.

The Prophet ﷺ was at his side, kissed his forehead, and said:

"The dunya took nothing from you, and you took nothing from it."

**Hold the world loosely.**

Uthman guarded his heart from it. Khawla gave good that asked for nothing back.

May Allah be pleased with them.
social.caption:
They were not from the powerful houses of Makkah, but Khawla bint Hakim and Uthman ibn Madhun (may Allah be pleased with them) were among the very first to believe.

Uthman refused wine when almost no one did, gave up his protection so he could suffer alongside the weak, and became the first of the migrants to be buried in Madinah. Over him the Prophet ﷺ said the world took nothing from him. Khawla, his wife, quietly arranged two of the most consequential marriages in Islam and never asked for the credit.

Which part of their story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

email.subject: The man the world could not touch
email.alt_subject: "The protection of Allah is better than yours"
email.preview: Two of the first to believe: a husband who gave up his safety, and a wife who shaped a household.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

They were not from the great tribes of Makkah. They held no banner that frightened anyone. Yet Khawla bint Hakim and Uthman ibn Madhun (may Allah be pleased with them) were among the very first people to believe, some say the fourteenth and fifteenth.

Uthman was unusual long before Islam reached him. In a time when almost everyone drank, he would not touch wine. Why, he asked, would I drink something that takes away my mind and lets those beneath me in character laugh at me?

When the persecution began, a powerful man placed Uthman under his protection, and no one dared touch him. But he could not bear to walk safe while the weak among the believers were being tortured. So he went and gave the protection back. I want to feel what they feel, he said. Soon after, he was struck hard in the eye in a quarrel near the Kaaba. His only regret was that the other eye had not been struck too, for the sake of Allah.

His wife, Khawla, carried her faith differently. She was known for her intelligence, her knowledge of medicine, and above all as the matchmaker of Makkah. When Khadijah passed away and the Prophet's home fell into grief, it was Khawla who gently arranged his marriages to Sawda and to Aisha. Every word we carry today from our mother Aisha traces back, in part, to a woman who simply brought two people together and never asked for the credit.

Uthman did not live long in Madinah. He was the first of the migrants to die there, and the Prophet ﷺ was at his side as his soul left him. He kissed Uthman on the forehead and said words that have never been forgotten: the dunya took nothing from you, and you took nothing from it.

There is something in both of them worth carrying. Refuse one small thing that would dull you. Do one good thing no one will ever know about. Hold the world a little more loosely.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: They were not from the great tribes of Makkah, and they held no banner that frightened anyone. Yet when the first believers were counted, Khawla bint Hakim and her husband Uthman ibn Madhun (may Allah be pleased with them) sat near the very top of the list.

Uthman was unusual even before Islam. In a city where almost everyone drank, he would not touch wine. Why would I take something that steals my mind, he said, and let lesser people laugh at me? He guarded his dignity when no command told him to.

When the believers began to suffer, a powerful man placed Uthman under his protection so that no one could harm him. But he watched the weak being tortured while he walked safe, and it troubled him. So he gave the protection back. The protection of Allah, he said, is better than yours.

His wife, Khawla, was the trusted friend of the Prophet's household and the matchmaker of Makkah. When Khadijah passed away and the home fell into grief, it was Khawla who gently arranged the Prophet's marriages to Sawda and to Aisha, and then quietly stepped back, asking for nothing.

One day, sitting with the Prophet ﷺ, Uthman watched him raise his eyes to the sky and listen to something unseen. Jibril had brought a verse, the one recited from every pulpit on Friday since. Uthman said that in that moment faith overwhelmed his heart, and he loved the Prophet ﷺ as never before.

He did not live long in Madinah. He was the first of the migrants to die there, and the Prophet ﷺ was at his side when his soul left him. He kissed Uthman on the forehead, marked his grave with a stone, and called it the grave of my brother.

Over him he said: the dunya took nothing from you, and you took nothing from it. Khawla lived on as a narrator of hadith and a teacher, remembered as the righteous woman, while Uthman was remembered as a man a river of good deeds still flowed toward in his grave.
storybook.pullquote: The dunya took nothing from you, and you took nothing from it.
storybook.reflection: Two people in one home showed two ways to hold the same faith. Uthman kept his heart so clean the world could not rearrange it. Khawla did good that asked for nothing and outlived her by centuries. We are tested the same way: in the small things we refuse, and the quiet good we do when no one is watching.
storybook.source: Their story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
